options.label: Consider RT to lesions with LCT
options.recommendation: RT can be used for localized transformed lesions. Concurrent management of co-existing disease based on clinical stage [MFSS-12].

options.label: Concurrent management of co-existing MF based on clinical stage
options.recommendation: Continue other treatment modalities used prior to transformation. If inadequate response or refractory, use Table 6 regimens for LCT or Table 7 regimens, consider allogeneic HCT [MFSS-12].

adjuvant_logic.summary: CR/PR: observe for recurrence. If relapse with limited lesions, consider retreatment. If relapse with generalized or extracutaneous, manage as generalized LCT.

nodes.criteria: Generalized cutaneous or extracutaneous lesions with LCT

options.label: Table 6 - Suggested Regimens for MF with LCT: Preferred: brentuximab vedotin, gemcitabine, liposomal doxorubicin, pralatrexate, romidepsin. Multiagent chemotherapy regimens (PTCL-NOS regimens). Other recommended: mogamulizumab, pembrolizumab
options.recommendation: Systemic therapy is primary. Brentuximab vedotin is effective in CD30+ LCT. Single agents preferred over combination due to toxicity. Multiagent chemotherapy reserved for refractory disease or significant extracutaneous disease [MFSS-12, MFSS-A 8 of 12].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Repeat imaging as clinically indicated. CR/PR: observe for recurrence. Inadequate response/refractory: use Table 7 regimens, consider allogeneic HCT, or clinical trial [MFSS-12].

management_pathways.title: Primary Cutaneous Anaplastic Large Cell Lymphoma (PC-ALCL)
branching_basis: Extent of disease: solitary/grouped vs multifocal lesions vs regional node involvement
CD30 positivity (>75% cells required for diagnosis)

options.label: ISRT (24 Gy curative; doses as low as 6 Gy used at some institutions; palliative: 2 Gy x 2 or 4 Gy x 1)
options.recommendation: RT alone or after excision. cCR rates ~95% with RT. Low-dose RT (≤20 Gy) also effective [PCTLD-4, PCLYM-A 3 of 3].

options.label: Surgical excision ± ISRT
options.recommendation: Small lesions may be excised with minimal non-disfiguring surgery [PCTLD-4].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Observe for recurrence. If relapse, retreat with initial treatment if skin-confined; for multifocal or extracutaneous, see multifocal lesions pathway [PCTLD-4].

options.label: Preferred: brentuximab vedotin. Other recommended: methotrexate (≤50 mg weekly), systemic retinoid (bexarotene), pralatrexate. Useful: observation (if asymptomatic), interferon (cat 3)
options.recommendation: Brentuximab vedotin is preferred based on ALCANZA (ORR4 75% vs 20% for physician's choice in PC-ALCL subgroup). Limited data for other options [PCTLD-4, PCTLD-A].

adjuvant_logic.summary: CR/PR: observe for recurrence. No response/refractory: clinical trial, retreat with same regimen (unless refractory/intolerant), or brentuximab vedotin if not used [PCTLD-4].

nodes.criteria: Regional node involvement (N1, biopsy-proven, excludes systemic ALCL)

options.label: Preferred: brentuximab vedotin ± ISRT, or ISRT alone in selected cases. Other recommended: brentuximab vedotin + CHP, methotrexate ± ISRT, pralatrexate ± ISRT, CHOP or CHOEP ± ISRT in selected cases
options.recommendation: ISRT to include LN(s) ± primary skin lesions. For regional node involvement, multiagent chemotherapy may be considered but brentuximab vedotin-based options are preferred [PCTLD-4, PCTLD-A].

adjuvant_logic.summary: CR/PR: observe for recurrence. No response/refractory: alternative regimen not used for primary treatment, or systemic therapies for MF with LCT (Table 6) [PCTLD-4].

management_pathways.title: Lymphomatoid Papulosis (LyP)
branching_basis: Extent of lesions: limited vs extensive
Presence of symptoms
Association with other lymphomas

options.label: Observation (preferred)
options.recommendation: LyP is not malignant; spontaneous regression is common. Observation is appropriate for asymptomatic patients [PCTLD-5].

options.label: Topical steroids
options.recommendation: For symptomatic limited lesions [PCTLD-5].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Continue current management. If no response/refractory, consider alternative or clinical trial.

options.label: Topical steroids
NB-UVB (preferred over PUVA)
options.recommendation: Phototherapy is effective for LyP [PCTLD-5].

adjuvant_logic.summary: If no response/refractory, consider alternative regimens or clinical trial.

options.label: Observation (preferred for asymptomatic)
Methotrexate (10-35 mg weekly), phototherapy (NB-UVB), systemic retinoids (bexarotene), topical steroids, topical mechlorethamine
options.recommendation: Low-dose methotrexate is widely used. Retinoids have limited data [PCTLD-5].

adjuvant_logic.summary: CR/PR: observe for recurrence. No response/refractory: use alternative regimen not used for primary treatment, or clinical trial. For refractory, brentuximab vedotin is an option based on phase II data (ORR 100%, CR 58%) [PCTLD-5, PCTLD-A].

management_pathways.title: Subcutaneous Panniculitis-Like T-Cell Lymphoma (SPTCL) with HLH, Systemic Disease, or High Tumor Burden
branching_basis: Presence of hemophagocytic lymphohistiocytosis (HLH)
High tumor burden (widespread subcutaneous disease)
Response to first-line therapy

options.label: Cyclosporine (≥5 mg/kg/day, typically initiated at 3-5 mg/kg/day in divided doses), pralatrexate, romidepsin
options.recommendation: Start with etoposide-based regimens to control HLH first, then move to disease-specific therapies. Single agents with or without prednisone are options [SPTCL-2].

options.label: Etoposide-based: CHOEP, DA-EPOCH, ESHA + platinum (cisplatin or oxaliplatin), ICE. See NCCN T-Cell Lymphomas PTCL-B.
options.recommendation: Consider for patients eligible for transplant. Etoposide-based combinations are preferred to control HLH [SPTCL-2].

options.label: ISRT (up to 40 Gy) for single lesion or limited disease
options.recommendation: For localized disease with or without HLH [SPTCL-2].

adjuvant_logic.summary: CR/PR: maintenance with single agents (cyclosporine, pralatrexate, romidepsin) or observation. Relapse: retreat with same regimen or alternate not used in first-line. Inadequate response after multiple therapies: consider allogeneic HCT or clinical trial [SPTCL-2].

management_pathways.title: Subcutaneous Panniculitis-Like T-Cell Lymphoma (SPTCL) without HLH and Low Tumor Burden
branching_basis: Absence of HLH
Low tumor burden (localized or limited subcutaneous disease)
Response to first-line therapy

options.label: Cyclosporine (≥5 mg/kg/day), methotrexate (PO/SC/IM), bexarotene (PO)
options.recommendation: Immunomodulatory agents are effective. Cyclosporine and methotrexate have shown high response rates. Dose adjustment based on response and tolerance [SPTCL-3].

options.label: ISRT or intralesional steroids for single lesion or limited disease
options.recommendation: For localized disease without HLH [SPTCL-3].

adjuvant_logic.summary: CR/PR: maintenance with single agents (cyclosporine, methotrexate, bexarotene) or observation. Relapse: retreatment with same regimen or alternate not used in first-line. Inadequate response: local therapy (if not used), or alternate regimens, or consider pralatrexate/romidepsin ± prednisone. Progressive disease (with HLH/systemic/high tumor burden): manage per SPTCL-2 pathway [SPTCL-3].

items.text: History and complete physical examination including complete skin examination (assessment of % BSA and type of lesion: patch/plaque/tumor/erythroderma), palpation of peripheral lymph node regions, palpation for organomegaly/masses
items.recommendation: Essential for all subtypes: PC-BCL, MF/SS, PCTLD, SPTCL, indolent LPD

items.text: Assessment of performance status and comorbidities
items.recommendation: Essential; influences treatment choice

items.text: Assess for distress using NCCN Distress Thermometer and Problem List, including social determinants of health
items.recommendation: Essential for all subtypes (CUTB-1, MFSS-2, PCTLD-2, PCTLD-3, SPTCL-1)

items.text: Fertility preservation counseling (sperm banking, IVF, ovarian tissue or oocyte cryopreservation) if chemotherapy or RT planned
items.recommendation: Essential for patients of childbearing potential (CUTB-1, MFSS-2, PCTLD-2, PCTLD-3)

items.text: Chest/abdomen/pelvis CT with contrast and/or FDG-PET/CT scan
items.recommendation: Essential for PC-BCL (may be omitted if clinically indicated for indolent). For MF/SS: integrated whole body FDG-PET/CT preferred or C/A/P CT with contrast for ≥T2b or LCT or FMF, or with palpable adenopathy or abnormal labs; consider for T2a (MFSS-2). For PC-ALCL: C/A/P CT or whole body FDG-PET/CT including arms/legs when needed (PCTLD-2). For SPTCL: whole body FDG-PET/CT to assess extracutaneous involvement (SPTCL-1). For LyP: imaging only if suspicion of associated lymphoma (PCTLD-3). For indolent LPD: imaging can be considered to exclude systemic lymphoma but not needed for typical cases.

items.text: Neck CT with contrast if whole body FDG-PET/CT not done
items.recommendation: Useful in certain circumstances for MF/SS and PC-ALCL

items.text: CBC with differential and absolute lymphocyte count
items.recommendation: Essential for all subtypes

items.text: Comprehensive metabolic panel
items.recommendation: Essential

items.text: Lactate dehydrogenase (LDH)
items.recommendation: Essential for all subtypes; elevated LDH is a prognostic factor in MF/SS

items.text: Flow cytometry of peripheral blood to assess and quantitate expanded T-cell population with aberrant phenotype (CD4/CD26-, CD4/CD7-, TRBC1)
items.recommendation: Essential for MF/SS (optional for T1; recommended for T2-4 or suspected extracutaneous disease). Useful in certain circumstances for SPTCL to rule out other T-cell lymphoma subtypes.

items.text: Clonal TRB and TRG gene rearrangement in peripheral blood lymphocytes if blood involvement suspected
items.recommendation: Essential for MF/SS when blood involvement suspected; also useful for PCTLD and SPTCL in selected cases

items.text: SPEP/quantitative immunoglobulins for PCMZL
items.recommendation: Useful in certain circumstances for PC-BCL

items.text: Ferritin, HLH workup (if B symptoms or HLH suspected)
items.recommendation: Essential for SPTCL; ferritin is a reliable marker for disease evolution

items.text: HTLV-1/2 serology
items.recommendation: Useful in certain circumstances for MF/SS and PCTLD to exclude ATLL; results can impact therapy (MFSS-1, PCTLD-1)

items.text: HIV, hepatitis B and C testing
items.recommendation: Essential for PC-BCL; hepatitis B testing due to risk of reactivation with immunotherapy + chemo

items.text: Biopsy of suspicious skin sites (punch, incisional, or excisional; shave biopsy not preferred for PC-BCL due to deep dermal infiltrates). Multiple biopsies may be necessary to capture pathologic variability
items.recommendation: Essential for all subtypes. For SPTCL, deep subcutaneous biopsy with adequate adipose tissue (wedge excision or deep telescope punch biopsy) is required.

items.text: Review of slides by a pathologist with expertise in cutaneous lymphoma diagnosis. Rebiopsy if findings are non-diagnostic or discordant with clinical presentation
items.recommendation: Essential for all subtypes

items.text: Adequate immunophenotyping by IHC panel: For PC-BCL includes CD20, CD3, CD10, BCL2, BCL6, IRF4/MUM1. For MF/SS includes CD2, CD3, CD4, CD5, CD7, CD8, CD20, CD30. For PCTLD includes CD3, CD4, CD8, CD20, CD30, CD56, ALK. For SPTCL includes TCR beta, TCR delta, CD2, CD3, CD20, CD4, CD5, CD7, CD8, CD30, CD56.
items.recommendation: Essential for diagnosis and subtyping

items.text: Molecular analysis for clonal TRB and TRG gene rearrangements
items.recommendation: Essential for MF/SS and SPTCL; useful in certain circumstances for PC-BCL (IgH rearrangement for clonality) and PCTLD

items.text: FISH or multigene panel testing (MGPT) for specific mutations: MYD88 and CD79B for PC-BCL (to distinguish PC-DLBCL leg type from PCFCL); t(14;18) if systemic FL suspected; ALK and DUSP22 rearrangements for PCTLD; JAK2 rearrangements in CTCL; HAVCR2 germline testing for SPTCL
items.recommendation: Useful in certain circumstances to refine diagnosis, prognosis, and therapeutic decisions

items.text: EBER-ISH (Epstein-Barr virus)
items.recommendation: Useful in certain circumstances for PC-BCL, PCTLD, SPTCL to rule out EBV-associated lymphomas

pretreatment_evaluation.category: Bone Marrow and Lymph Node Evaluation

items.text: Bone marrow biopsy
items.recommendation: Essential for PC-DLBCL leg type. Often reserved for patients with unexplained cytopenias or clinical suspicion of other subtypes in PC-BCL. For MF/SS: not required but may be helpful for unexplained hematologic abnormality. For PCTLD: optional for solitary cutaneous ALCL or without extracutaneous involvement. For SPTCL: consider if unexplained cytopenias or HLH. Not done for typical LyP or indolent LPD.

items.text: Excisional or core needle biopsy of enlarged lymph nodes or suspected extracutaneous sites. FNA alone is insufficient for initial lymphoma diagnosis.
items.recommendation: Essential for MF/SS and PC-ALCL when nodes are clinically abnormal (>1.5 cm). For SPTCL: consider biopsy of FDG-avid extracutaneous sites.

surgery.role: Surgery has a limited but defined role in the management of cutaneous lymphomas, primarily for diagnostic biopsy and for curative intent in selected solitary lesions. Surgical excision is not a primary modality for most cutaneous lymphomas due to their multifocal nature and radiosensitivity. However, for solitary/regional indolent B-cell lymphomas (PCFCL, PCMZL) and solitary PC-ALCL, excision may be considered as an alternative to RT, especially for small lesions where minimal non-disfiguring surgery is feasible. For LyP, surgery is not indicated due to spontaneous regression. For MF/SS, surgery is not a standard treatment except for biopsy or excision of solitary nodes for staging. For SPTCL, surgery is limited to diagnostic biopsy. For primary cutaneous indolent T-cell LPD, excision can be considered for symptomatic solitary lesions.
principles: Surgical excision should be minimal and non-disfiguring, particularly for cosmetically sensitive areas [CUTB-2, PCTLD-4].
Excision is typically reserved for small solitary lesions where complete removal is achievable with acceptable cosmetic outcome.
Post-excision, close observation for recurrence is warranted; many lesions may recur locally, and RT can be used as salvage.
For PC-ALCL, surgical excision alone or combined with ISRT is an option for solitary/grouped lesions [PCTLD-4].
For PC-BCL, excision is an option for solitary/regional disease, but local ISRT is the preferred initial treatment due to higher response rates and lower recurrence at the initial site [CUTB-2].
In SPTCL, surgery is not therapeutic except for diagnostic biopsy due to the deep subcutaneous nature of the disease.
For MF/SS, surgery is not used as primary therapy; skin biopsies are essential for diagnosis and monitoring.

procedures.name: Diagnostic skin biopsy
procedures.approach: Punch, incisional, or excisional biopsy of suspicious skin lesions. For SPTCL, deep telescope punch biopsy or wedge excision to obtain adequate adipose tissue. Multiple biopsies may be necessary to capture pathologic variability.
procedures.indications: Essential for diagnosis of all cutaneous lymphomas.
procedures.key_complications: Bleeding, infection, scarring. Inadequate sampling may lead to misdiagnosis, especially in deep dermal/subcutaneous processes.

procedures.name: Surgical excision of solitary lesions
procedures.approach: Complete excision with minimal margins for small solitary lesions, e.g., in PC-BCL or PC-ALCL. Local anesthesia, simple closure.
procedures.indications: Selected solitary/regional indolent B-cell lymphomas (PCFCL, PCMZL) and solitary PC-ALCL where RT is not desired or feasible.
procedures.key_complications: Local recurrence (higher than with RT in some studies), cosmetic deformity, infection.

procedures.name: Excisional lymph node biopsy
procedures.approach: Complete excision of a clinically abnormal lymph node (>1.5 cm) for histologic evaluation.
procedures.indications: Essential for staging in MF/SS and PC-ALCL with regional node involvement; to confirm or rule out lymphoma involvement.
procedures.key_complications: Bleeding, infection, nerve injury (depending on site).

surgery.resectability_criteria: Surgery is generally considered for solitary, well-circumscribed lesions that can be removed with clear margins without significant functional or cosmetic compromise. For PC-BCL, T1 lesions (solitary <5 cm or solitary ≥5 cm) are potentially resectable, but RT is preferred due to lower recurrence rates. For PC-ALCL, solitary lesions up to a few centimeters are suitable for excision. In MF/SS, surgical resection of skin lesions is not standard because of the diffuse nature of the disease. For indolent T-cell LPD, solitary nodules/plaques that are symptomatic may be excised.

radiation_therapy.role: Radiation therapy (RT) is a cornerstone of treatment for cutaneous lymphomas, used with curative intent for localized disease (e.g., unilesional MF, solitary PC-ALCL, indolent PC-BCL) and with palliative intent for symptom relief, disease control, and as part of combined modality therapy. RT is highly effective due to the radiosensitivity of lymphoid cells. The general intent is to treat evident skin disease with adequate margin both circumferentially and in depth. Techniques include external beam RT (EBRT) with photons, electrons, or low-energy x-rays. Total skin electron beam therapy (TSEBT) is a specialized technique for widespread skin involvement in MF [PCLYM-A].
principles: ISRT is recommended as the appropriate field for treating primary cutaneous lymphomas. Planning may require careful physical exam, ultrasound, or CT-based simulation [PCLYM-A 1 of 3].
The visible or palpable disease defines the gross tumor volume (GTV); CTV expands 1-2 cm circumferentially and in depth, not into intact bone. PTV accounts for setup variations [PCLYM-A 1 of 3].

For cutaneous lesions, surface margins of 1.0-1.5 cm are generally adequate. Depth margins should include all involved tissue. Treatment with 6-9 MeV electrons (with surface bolus) or low-energy x-rays (~100 Kv) provides adequate depth [PCLYM-A 2 of 3].
For nodal disease, ISRT principles follow NCCN T-Cell or B-Cell Lymphoma guidelines [PCLYM-A 1 of 3].

TSEBT techniques cover the entire cutaneous surface; patients are treated standing on a rotating platform or with multiple body positions. Shadowed areas may need supplemental electron fields. 'Shadowed' areas are supplemented individually [PCLYM-A 2 of 3].

TSEBT is common practice to follow with systemic therapies to maintain response. Limited safety data exist for combining TSEBT with systemic retinoids, HDAC inhibitors, or mogamulizumab [MFSS-A 2 of 12].

dose_frameworks.name: PCFCL/PCMZL curative dose
dose_frameworks.indication: Optimal initial management for solitary/regional disease. Lower doses (4 Gy) may be used initially with supplemental RT (4-20 Gy) for inadequate response, though data limited [PCLYM-A 2 of 3].

dose_frameworks.name: PCFCL/PCMZL palliative/relapsed dose
dose_frameworks.indication: For relapsed disease; may be adequate for palliation [PCLYM-A 2 of 3].

dose_frameworks.name: MF/SS individual plaque/tumor - low dose palliative
dose_frameworks.dose_per_fraction: 3-5 Gy (preferred depending on skin condition and volume; up to 8 Gy in single fraction may be used)
dose_frameworks.schedule: Low-dose RT with palliative intent, usually as combined modality therapy. Some institutions explore 4 Gy [PCLYM-A 2 of 3].

dose_frameworks.name: MF/SS unilesional presentation - definitive dose
dose_frameworks.indication: For rare initial unilesional lesions, given as monotherapy with curative intent [PCLYM-A 2 of 3].

dose_frameworks.name: TSEBT for MF/SS
dose_frameworks.fractions: Variable (e.g., 12 Gy in 2-3 weeks, 30-36 Gy over 6-9 weeks)
dose_frameworks.schedule: Variety of techniques; common dose ~12 Gy. Higher doses (24-36 Gy) for more extensive or refractory disease. Lower dose has fewer short-term complications and better ability to retreat [PCLYM-A 2 of 3].

dose_frameworks.name: PC-ALCL curative dose
dose_frameworks.indication: RT for curative treatment. Doses as low as 6 Gy used at some institutions, but data limited [PCLYM-A 3 of 3].

dose_frameworks.name: SPTCL curative dose
dose_frameworks.schedule: Adequate dose is uncertain; consensus organizations recommend up to 40 Gy [PCLYM-A 3 of 3].

approaches.name: Local ISRT for cutaneous lesions
approaches.dose_fractionation: Varies by histology: PC-BCL 24 Gy (4-20 Gy supplement for lower initial doses); MF plaque/tumor 8-12 Gy (24-30 Gy for unilesional); PC-ALCL 24 Gy; SPTCL up to 40 Gy
approaches.concurrent_chemotherapy: Not typically concurrent; sequential or combined modality in some settings (e.g., PC-DLBCL leg type, generalized MF/SS with LCT). Limited safety data for combination with systemic retinoids, HDAC inhibitors, or mogamulizumab.
approaches.indication: Solitary or localized skin lesions across all subtypes.
approaches.key_trial: For MF: Thomas TO et al. (2013) reported 94% CR with single-fraction 8 Gy. For PC-ALCL: Million L et al. (2016) reported 95% cCR with RT alone. For PC-BCL: Senff NJ et al. (2007) reported 99% CR with RT.
approaches.toxicities: Skin erythema, desquamation, alopecia (reversible), fibrosis (with higher doses), hypopigmentation, telangiectasias. Risk of secondary skin cancers with long-term use, especially with higher doses and in combination with phototherapy.

approaches.name: TSEBT for MF/SS
approaches.dose_fractionation: 12-36 Gy over 2-9 weeks. Boosts of 4-12 Gy to individual tumors. Additional local treatment for recalcitrant sites after generalized treatment.
approaches.concurrent_chemotherapy: Not standard due to limited safety data. Can be sequenced with systemic therapies.
approaches.indication: Widespread skin involvement in MF/SS (stage IB-IVA), especially for generalized plaque/tumor disease. Also used as cytoreductive therapy before allogeneic HCT in selected patients.
approaches.key_trial: Hoppe RT et al. (2015) pooled analysis: ORR 88% with 12 Gy. Morris S et al. (2017): ORR 87% with 12 Gy, median PFS 13 months. Higher doses (30-36 Gy) associated with better outcomes in T2 disease but more toxicity.
approaches.toxicities: Acute: erythema, desquamation, edema, temporary alopecia, nail loss, fatigue. Late: skin atrophy, telangiectasias, chronic xerosis, increased risk of secondary skin cancers. Lower doses minimize acute toxicity. Antibiotic therapy recommended in erythrodermic patients due to infection risk.

systemic_therapy.overview: Systemic therapy is indicated for advanced-stage MF/SS (≥ stage IIB), patients with inadequate response to skin-directed therapy, multifocal PC-ALCL, PC-ALCL with regional node involvement, extensive LyP, SPTCL with HLH or high tumor burden, and PC-DLBCL leg type. For MF/SS, the treatment philosophy emphasizes sequential use of single agents with favorable toxicity profiles before moving to more immunosuppressive or multiagent regimens. FDA-approved agents for MF/SS include bexarotene, brentuximab vedotin, denileukin diftitox-cxdl, mogamulizumab, romidepsin, and vorinostat. Systemic therapy is often combined with skin-directed therapy to maximize responses in the skin compartment. Therapeutic responses vary across compartments; agents like mogamulizumab show greater efficacy in blood and skin than in lymph nodes, while HDAC inhibitors have variable responses. Therefore, treatment decisions are guided by dominant disease compartment and symptom burden [MFSS/INTRO-2]. For PC-ALCL, brentuximab vedotin is the preferred agent. For SPTCL, immunomodulatory agents (cyclosporine, methotrexate) are first-line for low burden, while etoposide-based chemotherapy is used for HLH. Multiagent chemotherapy is generally reserved for refractory disease or as a bridge to allogeneic HCT.

by_stage_and_setting.setting: MF/SS Stage IA (selected cases with blood involvement or inadequate skin-directed therapy)
by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Bexarotene, interferon alfa, or methotrexate (as systemic therapy + skin-directed therapy)
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: These agents can be tolerated long-term with manageable side effects. Bexarotene requires monitoring for hypertriglyceridemia and hypothyroidism. Peginterferon alfa-2a or ropeginterferon alfa-2b may substitute for interferon alfa [MFSS-A 3 of 12].
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Acitretin, ECP (for B1 blood involvement), interferon gamma-1b, isotretinoin

by_stage_and_setting.setting: MF/SS Stage IB-IIA (higher skin disease burden or inadequate response to skin-directed therapy)
by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Bexarotene, brentuximab vedotin (if CD30+, ≥10% or any level), interferon alfa, methotrexate, mogamulizumab, romidepsin, vorinostat
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: ALCANZA trial demonstrated superiority of brentuximab vedotin over methotrexate/bexarotene in CD30+ MF. MAVORIC trial showed mogamulizumab superior to vorinostat in previously treated MF/SS, with higher responses in blood-involved stages [MFSS-A 4 of 12].
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Acitretin, denileukin diftitox-cxdl, ECP, interferon gamma-1b, isotretinoin, alemtuzumab (cat 2B), gemcitabine (cat 2B), liposomal doxorubicin (cat 2B), pembrolizumab (cat 2B), pralatrexate (cat 2B)

by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Single agents: bexarotene, brentuximab vedotin, denileukin diftitox-cxdl, gemcitabine, interferon alfa. Combination: retinoid + interferon alfa
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Brentuximab vedotin effective in CD30+ MF. Denileukin diftitox-cxdl (Study 302) showed ORR 36% (by IRC) in relapsed/refractory MF. Gemcitabine and liposomal doxorubicin are active single-agent chemotherapies [MFSS-A 5 of 12].
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Pembrolizumab, vorinostat, liposomal doxorubicin, methotrexate, mogamulizumab, pralatrexate, romidepsin; for limited disease: local RT and/or skin-directed therapy

by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Single agents: bexarotene, brentuximab vedotin, ECP, interferon alfa, methotrexate, mogamulizumab, romidepsin. Combinations: ECP + interferon alfa or retinoid, ECP + interferon alfa + retinoid, retinoid + interferon alfa
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: ECP is particularly useful for patients with blood involvement. Mogamulizumab shows high response in blood compartment. Patients with erythroderma are at increased risk for secondary infections; systemic antibiotics may be needed [MFSS-A 6 of 12].
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Vorinostat; useful in certain circumstances: acitretin, alemtuzumab, denileukin diftitox, gemcitabine, interferon gamma-1b, isotretinoin, liposomal doxorubicin, pembrolizumab, pralatrexate

by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Low-intermediate burden: bexarotene, ECP, interferon alfa, methotrexate, mogamulizumab, romidepsin, vorinostat, or combinations. High burden: mogamulizumab, romidepsin, or combinations (ECP + interferon/retinoid)
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Mogamulizumab demonstrated superiority over vorinostat in MAVORIC, with ORR 37% in SS. Alemtuzumab (subcutaneous low dose) has activity but requires CMV monitoring and PJP prophylaxis [MFSS-A 7 of 12].
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Alemtuzumab, brentuximab vedotin, gemcitabine, liposomal doxorubicin, pembrolizumab, pralatrexate

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Non-Sézary stage IVA2 or visceral/solid organ disease (stage IVB)
by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Brentuximab vedotin, gemcitabine, liposomal doxorubicin, pralatrexate, romidepsin; multiagent chemotherapy regimens (PTCL-NOS)
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Single agents are preferred over combination chemotherapy due to toxicity. Multiagent chemotherapy is reserved for relapsed/refractory or extensive extracutaneous disease [MFSS-A 8 of 12].

by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Brentuximab vedotin, gemcitabine, liposomal doxorubicin, pralatrexate, romidepsin; multiagent chemotherapy (PTCL-NOS regimens)
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: LCT often requires more aggressive systemic therapy. CD30 expression is common; brentuximab vedotin is effective. Single agents preferred upfront [MFSS-A 8 of 12].

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Relapsed/refractory MF/SS to multiple prior therapies
by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Alemtuzumab, chlorambucil, cyclophosphamide, duvelisib, etoposide, lenalidomide, pembrolizumab, pentostatin, temozolomide (for CNS involvement), multiagent chemotherapy (PTCL-NOS)
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Duvelisib: initial dosing 15 mg daily with dose modification based on weight and tolerability. Lenalidomide: initial 10 mg daily, escalate to 25 mg daily as tolerated. Pembrolizumab: disease flare seen in erythrodermic MF/SS; rapid progression reported in HTLV-positive patients [MFSS-A 8-9 of 12].
ALCANZA trial demonstrated superior ORR4 (75% vs 20%) and PFS (median 17 vs 4 months) compared to physician's choice in PC-ALCL subgroup [PCTLD-4, PCTLD-A].
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Methotrexate (≤50 mg weekly), systemic retinoid (bexarotene), pralatrexate; observation if asymptomatic; interferon (cat 3)

by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Brentuximab vedotin ± ISRT; ISRT alone in selected cases
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Brentuximab vedotin plus CHP is an option (ECHELON-2 trial). Multiagent chemotherapy (CHOP/CHOEP) may be used in selected cases [PCTLD-4].
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Methotrexate ± ISRT, pralatrexate ± ISRT, CHOP/CHOEP ± ISRT

by_stage_and_setting.setting: SPTCL with HLH, systemic disease, or high tumor burden
by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Cyclosporine (≥5 mg/kg/day), pralatrexate, or romidepsin ± prednisone. Also etoposide-based combination chemotherapy (CHOEP, DA-EPOCH, ESHA + platinum, ICE) for patients eligible for transplant
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Start with etoposide-based regimens to control HLH first, then move to disease-specific therapies. Cyclosporine (3-5 mg/kg/day initially, can increase) has shown high response rates. Pralatrexate and romidepsin have demonstrated activity in SPTCL [SPTCL-2].

by_stage_and_setting.setting: SPTCL without HLH and low tumor burden
by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Cyclosporine (≥5 mg/kg/day), methotrexate (PO/SC/IM), or bexarotene (PO) ± prednisone
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Immunomodulatory agents are effective and less toxic than chemotherapy. Cyclosporine and methotrexate have shown ORR of 94% and 58% respectively in case series [SPTCL-3].
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Local therapy (ISRT, intralesional steroids); pralatrexate or romidepsin ± prednisone for progressive disease

key_regimens.name: Brentuximab vedotin (MF/SS and PC-ALCL)
key_regimens.setting: MF/SS stage IB-IV with CD30+ disease (any level); PC-ALCL multifocal or with regional nodes

drugs.name: Brentuximab vedotin
drugs.day: Every 3 weeks for up to 16 cycles

key_regimens.key_toxicities: Peripheral neuropathy (most common, 69% any grade; 86% resolved/improved at median 46 months follow-up), infusion-related reactions, neutropenia, fatigue. JC virus reactivation and progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy (PML) risk.

key_regimens.name: Mogamulizumab (MF/SS)
key_regimens.setting: MF/SS stage IB-IV, especially with blood involvement (B1, B2)

drugs.name: Mogamulizumab
drugs.day: Weekly during first 28-day cycle, then on days 1 and 15 of subsequent cycles

key_regimens.key_toxicities: Infusion-related reactions (37%), drug-induced skin eruption (25%; may mimic CTCL, requires biopsy to rule out progression), diarrhea (14%), pyrexia (4%), cellulitis (3%). Loss of CCR4 expression may confer resistance.

key_regimens.name: Denileukin diftitox-cxdl (MF/SS)

drugs.name: Denileukin diftitox-cxdl
drugs.day: Days 1-5 every 21 days, up to 8 cycles

key_regimens.key_toxicities: Capillary leak syndrome (20% any grade; 6% grade ≥3, typically first 2 cycles), infusion-related reactions (74%), hypersensitivity (68%), hepatotoxicity (36%). CD25 positivity not predictive of efficacy.

key_regimens.name: Romidepsin (MF/SS)
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Nausea, fatigue, thrombocytopenia, neutropenia, QTc prolongation (monitor with concomitant antiemetics). Compartment-specific ORR: skin 40%, erythroderma 35%, blood 32%, LN 27%. High rate of pruritus reduction.

key_regimens.name: Vorinostat (MF/SS)
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Diarrhea, nausea, fatigue, dehydration (risk in elderly), thrombocytopenia. Cumulative toxicities rare. Limited safety data with phototherapy.

key_regimens.name: ECP (MF/SS)
key_regimens.setting: MF/SS stage III-IV, especially with blood involvement
key_regimens.cycle_days: Varies; typically at least 6 months of therapy
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Venous access complications, hypotension, infection risk. Generally well tolerated. More effective in patients with blood involvement (B1 or B2).

key_regimens.name: Pembrolizumab (MF/SS)
key_regimens.setting: Relapsed/refractory MF/SS (≥ stage IIB), including LCT and visceral disease
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Disease flare (especially in erythrodermic MF/SS; distinguish from progression), rash, fatigue, pneumonitis. Rapid progression reported in HTLV-positive patients. ORR 38% in phase II study.

key_regimens.name: Cyclosporine A (SPTCL)

drugs.name: Cyclosporine
drugs.dose: 3-5 mg/kg/day initially (oral), may increase up to ≥5 mg/kg/day

key_regimens.cycle_days: Continuous; dose adjustment based on response and tolerance
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Nephrotoxicity, hypertension, tremor, hirsutism, gingival hyperplasia. Requires monitoring of trough levels and renal function. Slow tapering as tolerated for maintenance.

key_regimens.name: Gemcitabine (MF/SS and LCT)

drugs.name: Gemcitabine
drugs.dose: Various schedules (e.g., 1000 mg/m2 days 1,8,15 every 28 days; low-dose schedules also used)

key_regimens.key_toxicities: Myelosuppression, nausea, flu-like symptoms, rash. Single-agent preferred over combination due to lower toxicity.

key_regimens.name: Pralatrexate (MF/SS and LCT and SPTCL)
key_regimens.setting: Relapsed/refractory MF/SS, LCT, SPTCL refractory to prior therapies

drugs.name: Pralatrexate
drugs.dose: 15 mg/m2 (MF/SS) or 30 mg/m2 (PROPEL trial) weekly for 3 of 4 weeks
drugs.day: Weekly for 3 weeks, then 1 week rest

key_regimens.key_toxicities: Mucositis, fatigue, thrombocytopenia, anemia. Dose adjustments needed for toxicity; vitamin B12 and folate supplementation required.

treatment_response_assessment.title: Treatment Response Assessment in Cutaneous Lymphomas
treatment_response_assessment.timing: Response assessment should occur at regular intervals, tailored to the specific disease type, stage, and treatment modality. For MF/SS, assessment typically occurs every 1-3 months during active therapy and every 3-6 months during observation or maintenance. For PC-BCL, additional imaging is not routinely needed during treatment; end-of-treatment PET/CT or CT may be performed to assess response or if clinical suspicion of progression arises [CUTB-2]. For PC-ALCL, follow-up includes monitoring size and number of lesions; imaging repeated as clinically indicated. For LyP, lifelong follow-up is warranted due to high risk for second lymphoid malignancies [PCTLD-5]. For SPTCL, response assessment is guided by clinical exam, imaging (PET/CT), and laboratory markers (ferritin, LDH) especially if HLH was present.
response_logic: Patients with disease achieving a clinical benefit (CR or PR) and/or those responding to primary treatment should be considered for maintenance or tapering of regimens to optimize response duration [MFSS-6, MFSS-7, MFSS-8, MFSS-10, MFSS-11].

For MF/SS, responses can vary across compartments (skin, blood, lymph nodes, viscera). Treatment decisions to continue, switch, or combine therapies are frequently guided by clinical assessment of the dominant disease compartment and symptom burden rather than uniform response criteria [MFSS/INTRO-2].
Patients with PR and suboptimal quality of life should be treated with other or additional primary treatment options to improve response before moving to refractory disease treatment [MFSS/INTRO-2].
Disease relapse after discontinuation of therapy may respond to re-treatment with previous therapy [MFSS/INTRO-2].
For PC-BCL, additional imaging during treatment is not needed. PET/CT or C/A/P CT at end of treatment may be needed to assess response or if clinical suspicion of progressive disease [CUTB-2].

For PC-ALCL, patients with clinical benefit should be considered for maintenance or tapering. Relapsed disease often responds well to the same treatment. PR should be treated with other primary treatment options not received before [PCTLD-4].
For LyP, patients with clinical benefit should continue current management. If no response/refractory, alternative regimens or clinical trial are considered [PCTLD-5].

For SPTCL, patients with CR/PR after first-line therapy should be considered for maintenance or tapering. Relapse can be retreated with same regimen or alternate. Inadequate response after multiple therapies may require allogeneic HCT or clinical trial [SPTCL-2, SPTCL-3].
imaging_recommendations: MF/SS: Imaging (with modalities used in workup) indicated when suspicious of clinical extracutaneous disease (e.g., new significant adenopathy, abnormal labs, accelerated skin disease) [MFSS-7, MFSS-8, MFSS-10]. For stage IV disease, repeat imaging as clinically indicated based on distribution of disease [MFSS-11].

PC-BCL: Additional imaging during the course of treatment is not needed after negative initial staging. FDG-PET/CT or C/A/P CT with contrast at the end of treatment may be needed to assess response or if clinical suspicion of progressive disease [CUTB-2].

PC-ALCL: Imaging with CT or PET/CT is indicated when there is suspicion of extracutaneous disease (e.g., palpable nodes, systemic symptoms). For LyP, imaging is not done for typical cases; only if suspicion of associated lymphoma [PCTLD-2, PCTLD-3].
SPTCL: Whole body FDG-PET/CT is essential for initial staging and should be repeated as clinically indicated to assess response and detect extracutaneous involvement [SPTCL-1].
Indolent LPD: Surveillance imaging is not needed for confirmed cases. Imaging can be considered as part of diagnostic workup to exclude systemic lymphoma when clinically indicated [INCTLD-1].
biopsy_or_salvage_logic: MF/SS: Rebiopsy skin if suspicious of LCT or FMF (not previously confirmed pathologically) or aggressive clinical behavior [MFSS-2]. If drug-induced skin eruption (e.g., mogamulizumab-associated rash) mimics disease progression, skin biopsy with adequate immunohistochemical stains and clonality assessment is recommended to distinguish from lymphoma progression [MFSS-A, PCLYM-C discussion].
PC-BCL: Rebiopsy if pathologic findings are non-diagnostic or discordant with clinical presentation. For relapsed disease, rebiopsy may confirm persistent disease.

PC-ALCL: Biopsy of enlarged LN or suspected extracutaneous sites is essential. Excisional or core needle biopsy preferred; FNA insufficient for initial diagnosis. Rebiopsy if consult material is non-diagnostic [PCTLD-2].
SPTCL: Rebiopsy if pathologic findings are non-diagnostic or discordant. Consider rebiopsy if clinical suspicion of progression or transformation.

Salvage therapy logic: For MF/SS, patients with refractory disease to multiple prior therapies should be considered for clinical trial, Table 7 regimens (relapsed/refractory disease), or allogeneic HCT. For PC-ALCL, refractory disease to multiple prior therapies should be managed with systemic therapies recommended for MF with LCT (Table 6). For LyP, refractory disease can be treated with brentuximab vedotin if not previously used. For SPTCL, progressive disease to multiple lines may require allogeneic HCT or clinical trial.
